explanation_md: `static_cast<Level>(2)` produces the enumerator `high` (since `high` has underlying value 2). When `l + 1` is evaluated, the unscoped enumerator implicitly converts to `int`, giving `2 + 1 = 3`. The result is an `int`, so `std::cout` prints `3` with no surprise.

options.a: `std::cin >> code;` is not allowed; you must read into a `Color` directly.
options.b: The line `Color c { code };` fails to compile because brace initialization does not allow an implicit int-to-enum conversion.
options.c: Printing an enum with `std::cout` is illegal and triggers a compile error.
options.d: The enum should be declared inside `main` for `c` to be usable.

explanation_md: Going from an integer to an enumeration is not an implicit conversion; you must request it with `static_cast<Color>(code)`. Brace initialization is the strictest form and rejects this. The other directions are fine: reading into an `int` works (option a is wrong), and printing an unscoped enum implicitly converts it back to `int` (option c is wrong).

`static_cast<Pet>(99)` is a legal conversion: the language does not check that `99` corresponds to any named enumerator. The variable `p` ends up holding `99`. No `case` label matches that value, so the `default` branch fires and the program prints `unknown`. This is exactly the silent bug the lesson warns about — without the `default`, the program would have printed nothing.

prompt_md: Why does the language require `static_cast` for int-to-enum but allow enum-to-int without one?

options.a: Because the enum-to-int direction is more efficient, the compiler is allowed to skip the check.
options.b: Because every enumerator has a valid integer representation, but not every integer corresponds to a named enumerator — the int-to-enum direction risks producing a value no one wrote.
options.c: Because `static_cast` cannot convert an enum value to an integer.
options.d: Because the C++ standard chose this asymmetry to match the behavior of `std::cin` and `std::cout`.

explanation_md: Going from an enumerator to its underlying integer is always safe — the value exists by construction. Going the other way is dangerous: any `int` is potentially being treated as an enumerator, including values outside the listed names. Requiring an explicit cast forces you, the author, to acknowledge the risk.
